section.title: Greeting
section.sectionRationale: Peter opens his farewell letter the way he means to continue it, with the person of Jesus Christ at the center. In two verses he names himself, names his readers, and names the source of everything that follows: the righteousness of God our Savior and the true knowledge of Him.
section.thesis: The same precious faith belongs equally to every believer, given through the righteousness of our God and Savior Jesus Christ, and grace and peace grow in us through the knowledge of Him.
section.frameNote: One movement in two parts: the writer and his readers, verse 1, and the blessing of grace and peace through knowledge, verse 2.

verses.text: Simon Peter, a {{doulos|servant}} and {{apostolos|apostle}} of Jesus Christ, To those who through the {{dikaiosyne|righteousness}} of our God and {{soter|Savior}} Jesus Christ have received {{isotimos|a faith as precious as ours}}:
Grace and peace be multiplied to you through the {{epignosis|knowledge}} of God and of Jesus our Lord.

doulos.gloss: A slave or bondservant, one wholly owned by and devoted to a master. Peter names himself a doulos before he names himself an apostle. The great apostle is first and always a servant of Jesus Christ, and so his authority rests on belonging entirely to his Lord.

apostolos.gloss: One sent out with a commission, an authorized representative. Peter writes as an apostle of Jesus Christ, sent and empowered to speak with His authority. This grounds the whole letter: what follows is not private opinion but the message of one Christ Himself appointed.

dikaiosyne.gloss: Righteousness, justice, the quality of being right and fair. Peter says the readers received their faith through the righteousness of our God and Savior. God's own fair and faithful character, not human merit, is why believers everywhere share an equal standing before Him.

soter.gloss: Savior, deliverer, rescuer. The phrase our God and Savior Jesus Christ, by a rule of Greek grammar, calls the one person Jesus both God and Savior. Peter opens by confessing the full deity of Christ, the very truth the false teachers will deny.

isotimos.gloss: Of equal value, equally precious, of the same honor. The faith the newest and humblest believer receives is of the same worth as that of Peter the apostle. No one in Christ holds a second-class faith; all share one precious standing given by God.

epignosis.gloss: Full, deep, personal knowledge, a recognition that goes beyond bare information. This word runs through 2 Peter as the antidote to false teaching. Grace and peace grow not by novelty but by an ever deeper knowledge of God and of Jesus our Lord.

basic.context: This is the opening of Peter's second and final letter, written as a farewell to churches facing false teachers and doubters. In just two verses he sets the tone: he is a servant and apostle of Jesus, his readers share the very same precious faith he has, and that faith comes through the righteousness of Jesus, who is called both God and Savior. He prays that grace and peace would grow in them through knowing Christ more deeply.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a real letter opening, and every phrase is loaded. The most important point is that Peter calls Jesus our God and Savior in one breath, a plain confession of Christ's deity right where the false teachers would later deny the Master. He also insists that ordinary believers hold a faith equal in value to an apostle's, so no elite class of teachers can lord it over them.
basic.originalLanguages: Peter is a doulos, a bondservant, before he is an apostolos, a sent one. The readers received an isotimos faith, one of equal value to his own, through the dikaiosyne, the righteousness, of Jesus their God and soter, Savior. And grace and peace multiply through epignosis, deep personal knowledge of God, the key word of the whole letter.
basic.typology: Peter's self-description as servant echoes the servants of God in the Old Testament, Moses, David, and the prophets, all sent to speak God's word. He stands in that line, now sent by the risen Christ, and points forward to the true knowledge of God that the new covenant pours out on all His people.
basic.christ: The greeting is saturated with Christ. He is the Master whose servant Peter is, the one whose righteousness secures the believer's faith, and, most strikingly, He is called our God and Savior. From the first line, Jesus is confessed as fully God and the only rescuer, the truth the rest of the letter will defend.
basic.god: God gives a faith of equal worth to all who believe, showing His impartial grace. He is righteous, so that faith rests on His fair and faithful character, not on human achievement. And He is generous, multiplying grace and peace to those who seek to know Him.
basic.crossReferences: First Peter opens with a similar greeting of grace and peace. Titus 2:13 also calls Jesus our great God and Savior. Romans 1:1 shows Paul, like Peter, calling himself a servant and apostle. And John 17:3 defines eternal life as knowing God and Jesus Christ.
basic.oneStory: God has always called servants to carry His word, and always given faith as a gift of grace. Peter, a servant of the risen Christ, greets a church that shares the one precious faith of all God's people across the ages, a faith secured by the righteousness of Jesus, God and Savior, in whom the whole story finds its center.

context.literary: A standard ancient letter opening, writer to readers with a greeting, but every element is weighted toward the letter's themes. Naming Jesus as God and Savior, stressing an equal faith, and highlighting knowledge all preview the coming battle against false teachers who deny the Master and peddle a false, elite wisdom.
context.historical: Peter writes near the end of his life, likely from Rome in the mid 60s, to churches in Asia Minor already addressed in his first letter. False teachers are rising, so he opens by anchoring his readers in shared apostolic faith and in the true knowledge of Christ before he names the danger.
context.canonical: As the second of Peter's letters, this greeting joins the New Testament's chorus confessing Jesus as both God and Savior. It sets the note of true knowledge that will be sounded against counterfeit teaching, and it echoes the apostolic pattern of grace and peace that opens the epistles.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To establish Peter's authority as a servant and apostle of Christ, to assure ordinary believers that their faith is fully equal to an apostle's, and to root the whole letter in the deity of Jesus and the growing knowledge of Him.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The greeting mainly describes who Peter is, who his readers are, and what God has given them. Its abiding call is implicit but real: hold fast the one precious faith, confess Jesus as God and Savior, and pursue the knowledge of Him in which grace and peace grow.

debates.question: Does verse 1 call Jesus our God, or distinguish God from Jesus?

views.label: One person, our God and Savior Jesus Christ
views.case: The Granville Sharp rule of Greek grammar, where a single article governs two nouns joined by and, points to one person. Peter is confessing Jesus as both God and Savior, just as Titus 2:13 does. This reading fits the letter's aim of exalting Christ against those who deny Him.

views.label: Two distinct persons, of God and of the Savior
views.case: A few would read a distinction, our God, and our Savior Jesus Christ, paralleling the wording of verse 2. Yet even here Peter would affirm both God's righteousness and Christ's saving work as the ground of faith.

debates.bearing: The stronger grammar and the letter's Christ-exalting purpose favor reading Jesus as our God and Savior. Either way the passage confesses the saving righteousness that gives believers their faith, and the majority orthodox reading sees here a clear witness to the full deity of Christ.

debates.question: What does it mean that the readers received a faith as precious as ours?

views.label: Equal standing shared by all believers
views.case: The word isotimos means of equal value. The faith of every believer, including recent Gentile converts, is worth as much as that of Peter and the apostles. There is no spiritual aristocracy; all hold one precious faith by grace.

views.label: The same faith once delivered
views.case: Others stress that faith here means the body of truth believed, so all share the same apostolic gospel. This still guards against any group claiming a higher, secret faith than the one handed down.

debates.bearing: Both readings undercut the false teachers' pretensions to superior knowledge. Whether faith means personal trust or the common gospel, Peter insists it is one and equal for all, given by God and precious beyond price.

study.originalLanguages: The opening compresses the letter's theology into a few words. Peter is a doulos, a bondservant wholly owned by his Master, before he is an apostolos, a commissioned messenger. The readers hold an isotimos faith, of equal honor with his own, granted through the dikaiosyne, the righteousness, of Jesus their God and soter, Savior. By the Granville Sharp construction, that single phrase confesses Christ as both God and Savior. And the blessing flows through epignosis, the deep, personal knowledge of God that is the letter's great theme and the true safeguard against every counterfeit.

typology.fulfillment: Peter takes up the title borne by Moses, David, and the prophets, now as a servant of the risen Christ, showing the continuity of those God sends to speak His word.

typology.type: The knowledge of God promised in the new covenant
typology.fulfillment: Jeremiah 31:34 promised a day when all God's people would know Him; Peter grounds grace and peace in that knowledge of God and of Jesus, poured out in Christ.

study.christ: The greeting places Jesus Christ at the center from the first syllable. Peter is His servant and His apostle. It is the righteousness of Christ, our God and Savior, that secures the believer's precious faith, and the grammar of verse 1 confesses Jesus as God Himself. Grace and peace flow through the knowledge of Him. Here, before any argument begins, is the confession the whole letter defends: Jesus is fully God, the only Savior, and the true and living object of a faith shared equally by all His people.
study.god: God is revealed as righteous and impartial. He gives a faith of equal value to every believer, apostle and new convert alike, so that no one boasts and no one is excluded. His righteousness, not human merit, is the ground of that faith. And He is lavishly generous, multiplying grace and peace to all who press on to know Him. This is a God whose fairness and kindness shape the very first words of the letter.

crossReferences.note: Waiting for the blessed hope and the appearing of the glory of our great God and Savior Jesus Christ, the same confession of Christ's deity as in verse 1.
Peter's first letter opens with the same greeting of grace and peace to the same scattered churches.
Paul, like Peter, calls himself a servant and an apostle, set apart for the gospel of God.
Eternal life is to know the only true God and Jesus Christ whom He sent, the knowledge Peter blesses his readers with.

study.oneStory: From the beginning God has raised up servants to carry His word and has given faith as a gift of grace. Peter, a bondservant of the risen Christ, greets churches that share the one precious faith of all God's people through every age. That faith rests on the righteousness of Jesus, confessed here as God and Savior, and grows through the knowledge of Him. The letter's opening thus stands squarely on the whole Bible's story, which finds its center and its rescuer in Christ.

sermon.title: Grace and Peace
sermon.why: From Begg's series A Study in 2 Peter, this message opens the letter at 2 Peter 1:1-4, treating Peter's self-description, the precious faith shared by all believers, and the confession of Jesus as our God and Savior. A clear, orthodox handling of the greeting.

quiz.q: Why does Peter call himself a servant before he calls himself an apostle?

options.text: Because he is first of all owned by and devoted to Christ, and his authority rests on belonging to his Master.
options.note: Doulos, bondservant, comes first; apostolic authority flows from serving Christ.

options.text: Because being an apostle was unimportant to him.
options.note: Apostleship matters greatly; he names it too, but servanthood comes first.

options.text: To show he was merely one of many ordinary workers with no special role.
options.note: He is a servant, yet he also claims real apostolic commission and authority.

options.text: Because he was ashamed of being an apostle.
options.note: There is no shame here; he gladly names both titles under Christ.

quiz.q: What does Peter mean when he says his readers have received a faith as precious as ours?

options.text: Every believer's faith is of equal value to an apostle's; there is no spiritual aristocracy.
options.note: Isotimos means of equal honor; all share one precious faith by grace.

options.text: The readers' faith is the same apostolic gospel handed down to all.
options.note: True and related, but the word stresses equal value, not only shared content.

options.text: Apostles hold a higher grade of faith than ordinary Christians.
options.note: The opposite: Peter insists the faith is equal for all.

options.text: Faith is a reward earned by the most disciplined believers.
options.note: Faith is received as a gift through God's righteousness, not earned.

quiz.q: How does the greeting describe Jesus Christ?

options.text: As our God and Savior, whose righteousness secures the believer's faith.
options.note: Verse 1, by the Granville Sharp construction, confesses Jesus as God and Savior.

options.text: As a great teacher and example, but not as God.
options.note: Peter goes further, naming Jesus our God and Savior.

options.text: As Savior only, with no claim to deity.
options.note: He is Savior, but the same phrase also calls Him our God.
The greeting exalts Christ far above the prophets, as God and Savior.

quiz.q: How do grace and peace grow in the believer, according to verse 2?

options.text: Through the deep, personal knowledge of God and of Jesus our Lord.
options.note: Epignosis, true knowledge of Christ, is the channel and the letter's key theme.

options.text: Through gaining new and secret teachings beyond the gospel.
options.note: The false teachers offered novelty; Peter points to knowing Christ Himself.

options.text: By deeper acquaintance with the Lord we already trust.
options.note: True, though the verse names knowledge of God specifically as the channel.

options.text: By personal effort alone, apart from knowing God.
options.note: Effort matters later, but here grace and peace flow through knowledge of God.

questions: Peter is first a servant of Christ and only then an apostle. What would change if you saw your own identity first as one who belongs wholly to Jesus?
The newest believer holds a faith as precious as an apostle's. How does that truth free you from comparing your standing with others in the church?
Peter confesses Jesus as our God and Savior in his opening line. How does a settled confidence in the full deity of Christ steady your faith?
Grace and peace grow through knowing God more deeply. What is one concrete way you can pursue the knowledge of Christ this week rather than settling for what you already know?

translationNotes.note: A faith as precious as ours renders isotimos, of equal value or honor; the faith of every believer is worth as much as an apostle's.

Our God and Savior Jesus Christ follows a Greek construction (the Granville Sharp rule) that most naturally names one person, confessing Jesus as both God and Savior; a footnote tradition allows the alternative rendering that distinguishes God from the Savior.
Knowledge renders epignosis, a full and personal knowledge, a key term repeated through the letter as the safeguard against false teaching.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 2 Peter 1:1-2. No dashes appear in this movement's text.
